attachment.content:
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- spec-human: Create a human-reviewable architecture spec using a 6-section template (Summary, Background, Architecture, Interface, Edge Cases, Alternatives) BEFORE implementing a non-trivial design. Triggered explicitly via /spec-human <topic>, or when the user says "write a human spec", "create a review-ready design doc", "spec this out for review", or "I need a spec for X before we build it". Produces an ASCII markdown document at ./specs/<slug>.md aimed at peer engineers and tech leads who need to validate core architecture and trade-offs in 5 minutes. Do NOT auto-trigger on general design questions or coding tasks - this is a deliberate review gate that only fires on explicit request. For LLM-targeted specs (future sibling skill), use /spec-llm instead.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt:
In /local/nick/vsim, find what "DMI" means in this codebase. It could be Debug Module Interface (RISC-V) or Direct Memory Interface (SystemC TLM). Specifically:

1. Search src/, external/cpu_wrappers/, and src/cpu_wrappers/ for "dmi", "DMI", "debug_module", "transport_dbg", "get_direct_mem_ptr" to understand which one it is.

2. If it's Direct Memory Interface (DMI in TLM-2.0), find:
   - How CPU cores expose register access (general purpose registers, CSRs, vector registers)
   - Files that mention `dmi`, `vector`, `vreg`, `VRF`, `v0`, `v1`, etc.
   - Whether the verilated CPU core has separate signals/DPI for register peek

3. If it's RISC-V Debug Module Interface, find:
   - Debug module implementation files
   - How abstract commands access GPRs, FPRs, CSRs, vector registers
   - Files like debug_module.cpp/hpp, dmi.cpp/hpp

4. List file paths with line numbers showing the key interfaces/APIs. Especially look for the wrapper/interface layer that sits between SystemC and the verilated cores (in src/cpu_wrappers/ or similar).

Report concisely (under 400 words) what DMI means here, where it is, and what register types it currently exposes.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have the complete picture. Let me confirm by looking at lines 89-90:

Based on my analysis of the codebase, here's what I found:

## Summary

**DMI = RISC-V Debug Module Interface** (not Direct Memory Interface from TLM). 

This is accessed via **JTAG Debug Transport Module (DTM)** which exposes CPU registers through AHB over a 9-bit address space (512 bytes).

### Key Files and Architecture

**Core DMI implementation:** `/local/nick/vsim/src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` (lines 39-45, 69-100, 196-225)
- JTAG IR=0x11 selects DMI register
- DMI format: [status(2 bits) | addr(abits) | data(32 bits)] = 34 + 7 = 41 bits
- Operations: read (op=1), write (op=2) to abstract debug registers

**CPU-to-Platform Binding:** `/local/nick/vsim/src/simulator.hpp` (line 89-90)
```cpp
cpu.bind(platform.clk, platform.resetn, platform.reset_vector,
         platform.dmi_signals, ...)
```

**DMI Signal Interface:** `/local/nick/vsim/src/platform/platform.hpp` (lines 36-37, 49)
- AHB manager (9-bit addr, 32-bit data)
- Connected to JTAG DTM via TCP socket on port 9104

### Register Types Currently Exposed

All CPUs (d45, d45_se, a45, ax45mp, ax65, ax66, ax46mpv, nx45v) expose via **Verilated ports** (lines in `/local/nick/vsim/src/cpu_wrappers/*_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp`):

1. **General Purpose Registers (GPR)** - via `hart0_probe_selected_gpr_value` and `hart0_probe_gpr_index`
2. **Program Counter (PC)** - via `hart0_probe_current_pc`
3. **Vector Registers** (NX45V only) - likely through similar probe mechanism

The DMI itself doesn't directly expose these. Instead, the probe ports are SystemC signals that allow read-only access to internal CPU state. The DMI AHB address space (9 bits = 512 bytes) maps to debug module control registers per RISC-V spec, with data flowing through abstract commands.

**No CSR or FPR exposure documented** in the current DMI implementation—only GPR access via SystemC side-channel probes.

Based on my analysis of the codebase, here's what I found:

## Summary

**DMI = RISC-V Debug Module Interface** (not Direct Memory Interface from TLM). 

This is accessed via **JTAG Debug Transport Module (DTM)** which exposes CPU registers through AHB over a 9-bit address space (512 bytes).

### Key Files and Architecture

**Core DMI implementation:** `/local/nick/vsim/src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` (lines 39-45, 69-100, 196-225)

```cpp

```

The DMI itself doesn't directly expose these. Instead, the probe ports are SystemC signals that allow read-only access to internal CPU state. The DMI AHB address space (9 bits = 512 bytes) maps to debug module control registers per RISC-V spec, with data flowing through abstract commands.

#ifndef INTERNAL_SIGNALS_NX45V_HPP
#define INTERNAL_SIGNALS_NX45V_HPP

#include "vl_dot_name.hpp"

#include <Vnx45v_plain___024root.h>

// clang-format off
// Define core macros for constructing signals
#define IPIPE_SIGNAL(s) \
  VL_DOT_NAME(ae350_vcpu_cluster_subsystem, u_cluster, u_complex0, u_core0, \
              nx45v_core, kv_core, kv_ipipe, s)

#define CORE_SIGNAL(s) \
  VL_DOT_NAME(ae350_vcpu_cluster_subsystem, u_cluster, u_complex0, u_core0, \
              nx45v_core, kv_core, s)

#define VPU_SIGNAL(s) \
  VL_DOT_NAME(ae350_vcpu_cluster_subsystem, u_cluster, u_complex0, u_vpu, s)

#define VDEC_SIGNAL(...) \
  VL_DOT_NAME(ae350_vcpu_cluster_subsystem, u_cluster, u_complex0, u_vpu, u_vdis, __VA_ARGS__)

#include "common_signals_45.hpp"

// signal alias, signal name, right shift, mask
#define EXPAND_VPU_INTERNAL_SIGNALS \
  X(vpu_cmt_valid, VPU_SIGNAL(vpu_cmt_valid)) \
  X(vpu_srf_wvalid, VPU_SIGNAL(vpu_srf_wvalid)) \

input.description: Look for VRF storage backing in verilator root

lastPrompt: does DMI support vector registers? if not, could we extend it without changing the verilated cpu core?

aiTitle: Investigate Verilator DMI vector register support

input.command: grep -nE "u_vrf__DOT.*u_mem__DOT" /local/nick/vsim/build/Vax45mpv_premium/obj_sc/Vax45mpv_premium___024root.h | head -50 | sed 's/.*u_mem__DOT__//; s/;.*//' | sort -u | head -30

content.text:
**Short answer**: The vsim C++ "DMI" path (`src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` -> AHB -> the verilated core's `dmi_*` ports) is a transparent JTAG-to-AHB bridge - it forwards whatever you send, so vector-register support is decided entirely by the **RTL Debug Module inside the verilated core**, not by anything in this repo. The C++ side never decodes register numbers. The separate side-channel (`hart0_probe_*` at `src/cpu_wrappers/nx45v_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:181-185`) exposes only PC and GPR - no vector regs.

**Reasoning**

`jtag_dtm` (`src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp:118-225`) only:
1. shifts JTAG IR/DR,
2. on `update_dr` of the DMI register, calls `ahb.read/write(addr<<2, data)` (lines 211-225).

The DM register file (`dmcontrol`, `command`, `data0..N`, `progbuf*`, ...) lives inside RTL behind the `dmi_*` AHB slave. Abstract-command `Access Register` with `regno = 0x1020..0x103F` (v0..v31, per RISC-V Debug Spec 1.0) only works if the RTL DM implements it; vsim cannot add/remove that.

**Extending without touching RTL**

The DMI bus itself: **no** - the register decoder is in RTL, so you can't add new DMI registers from the C++ side.

But you can add a **side-channel peek** (same trick already used at `src/cpu_wrappers/nx45v_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:203-298` and `src/signals/internal_signals_nx45v.hpp`):

- Verilator exposes every internal signal via `rootp->ae350_vcpu_cluster_subsystem__DOT__u_cluster__DOT__u_complex0__DOT__u_vpu__DOT__u_vrf__DOT__...`. The VRF backing already appears in the generated root header (`build/Vax45mpv_premium/obj_sc/Vax45mpv_premium___024root.h`, e.g. line 6443 `u_vrf__DOT____Vcellinp__gen_vpu_mem__BRA__7__KET____DOT__u_mem__vrf_w1_data`).
- Add a `get_vreg(idx, element)` to the wrapper that reads those `rootp->` fields. Register it through `build_signal_registry()` so Python (`pybind11`) can read v0..v31.
- No `.v`/`.sv` change, no re-verilate of new ports.

**Caveats**
- VRF is striped across 8 lanes (`gen_vpu_lanes[0..7]`) and 8 memory banks (`gen_vpu_mem[0..7]`). You must reverse-engineer the banking/striping (lane = element % 8, bank = vreg-group, etc.) to reconstruct architectural `vN`. Confirm with a `vmv.v.x v1, x1` test.
- It is **read-only** and out-of-band from the real debugger - OpenOCD/GDB won't see it; only vsim-internal users (checkpoint/dump/Python REPL) will.
- If you need GDB to see vregs, that requires real DM support in RTL; no C++-only workaround.

Want me to prototype the side-channel `get_vreg` on `nx45v` (or `ax45mpv_premium` since it's already built), or first verify whether the RTL DM responds to `regno=0x1020` via the existing `jtag_dtm` so we know option 1 is off the table?

message.content: if DMI doesn't support vector register, how does GDB read and write the vector registers?

input.description: Look for progbuf and abstract command references

lastPrompt: if DMI doesn't support vector register, how does GDB read and write the vector registers?

aiTitle: Investigate Verilator DMI vector register support

content.text:
**Short answer**: GDB doesn't use the abstract "Access Register" command for vector regs - it uses the **Program Buffer (progbuf)** mechanism in the DM, which runs a tiny sequence of real RISC-V instructions on the halted hart.

**Why Access Register can't do it directly**

The standard regno encoding in the Debug Spec assigns:
- `0x1000-0x101F` -> GPR x0..x31
- `0x1020-0x103F` -> FPR f0..f31
- (no slot for v0..v31)

Vector regs are excluded because VLEN can be up to 65536 bits (8 KB) per register - they don't fit in `data0..N` scratch regs and have no fixed size at DM-build time.

**How GDB/OpenOCD actually do it**

1. Halt the hart (`dmcontrol.haltreq`).
2. Save scratch state: a few GPRs, plus `vtype`, `vl`, `vstart`, `mstatus.VS` (all via Access Register on CSRs/GPRs - which IS supported).
3. **Read v_n[element_i]**:
   - Put `i` into `vstart` (CSR write via abstract cmd).
   - Load progbuf with something like:
     ```
     vmv.x.s  a0, v_n     # element[vstart] -> a0
     ebreak
     ```
   - Set `command.postexec=1` and execute -> hart runs progbuf.
   - Read `a0` back via Access Register -> read `data0`.
   - Repeat for each element (or use `vslidedown` to step).
4. **Write v_n[element_i]**: symmetric - write `data0`, run `vmv.s.x v_n, a0` in progbuf with `vstart=i`.
5. Restore CSRs/GPRs, resume.

A faster variant: progbuf does `vse8.v v_n, (a0)` to dump the whole register to a scratch memory buffer, then DM's system-bus port (or another memory read) pulls the bytes out - one progbuf execution per register instead of per element.

**Implication for vsim**

If your goal is GDB inspecting vregs in vsim:
- It already works **iff** the Andes RTL DM implements progbuf (`abstractcs.progbufsize > 0`). The C++ `jtag_dtm` is a transparent transport and needs no changes.
- Verify by halting via OpenOCD and reading `abstractcs` (DMI addr 0x16) - check bits 28:24 (`progbufsize`). If non-zero, GDB `info reg v0` will Just Work.

If your goal is fast in-sim vector-state dump (checkpoint, Python introspection):
- Progbuf is **slow** - each element costs many JTAG cycles + a real hart resume.
- The side-channel `rootp->...u_vrf__DOT__...` peek I described last turn is orders of magnitude faster and doesn't perturb the hart (no halt, no clobbering GPRs/CSRs).

Which path do you want: confirm progbuf works in the RTL DM (test with OpenOCD), or prototype the side-channel peek?

**Progbuf = Program Buffer**: a small instruction memory inside the RISC-V Debug Module (typically 2-16 RV32 instructions). The debugger writes real RISC-V instructions into it, then tells the halted CPU to run them.

**How it works (concrete walk)**

The DM exposes these DMI registers (per Debug Spec):
- `data0..dataN` (DMI addr 0x04-...) - scratch values, like mailboxes between debugger and hart.
- `progbuf0..progbufN` (DMI addr 0x20-...) - the instruction slots.
- `command` (DMI addr 0x17) - tells DM what to do.
- `abstractcs` (DMI addr 0x16) - status, includes `progbufsize` field showing how many slots exist.

To read v0[0] for example:

1. Debugger writes 2 instructions into progbuf0/progbuf1:
   ```
   progbuf[0] = vmv.x.s a0, v0    # copy v0's first element into GPR a0
   progbuf[1] = ebreak            # return to debug mode
   ```
2. Debugger writes `command` register with "Access Register, regno=a0, postexec=1, transfer=0".
3. DM hardware:
   - jumps the halted hart to progbuf,
   - hart executes `vmv.x.s` and `ebreak`,
   - DM auto-copies `a0` -> `data0`.
4. Debugger reads DMI `data0` -> gets v0[0].

**Why it exists**

Abstract commands only handle simple, fixed-size accesses (GPR, FPR, CSR). Anything weird - vector regs, custom CSRs, cache flushes, atomic sequences - the debugger expresses as a short RISC-V program and ships it through progbuf. The DM doesn't need to know what the instructions mean; the hart runs them natively.

Think of it like: abstract command = pre-baked API, progbuf = "send me code to run".

message.content: how to understand those concept about DM any official speficiation

content.text:
**Official spec**: RISC-V External Debug Support Specification, maintained by the RISC-V External Debug Task Group.

- Repo (source + releases): `https://github.com/riscv/riscv-debug-spec`
- Released PDFs are in the `Releases` tab. Use **version 1.0.0** (ratified) if you can, or **0.13.2** which most existing tools (OpenOCD, GDB, your Andes RTL DM) actually implement.

**Reading order for understanding DM** (most useful first, skip the rest)

| Chapter / Section | What you learn | Why read it |
|---|---|---|
| Ch 1 - Introduction | Overall block diagram: Debugger -> DTM -> DMI -> DM -> Hart | The single picture that ties all terms together |
| Ch 6 - Debug Transport Module (JTAG) | TAP states, `dtmcs`, `dmi` JTAG registers | Maps to vsim's `src/platform/jtag_dtm.hpp` line-by-line |
| Ch 3.1-3.2 - DM overview | What "halt", "resume", "abstract command" mean | Mental model |
| Ch 3.14 - DM register listing | Every DMI address (`dmcontrol`, `abstractcs`, `command`, `data0..N`, `progbuf0..N`, ...) | Reference table you will reread often |
| Ch 3.6 - Abstract Commands | Access Register cmd, regno encoding (GPR 0x1000-0x101F, FPR 0x1020-0x103F, CSR 0x0000-0x0FFF) | Explains why vregs are NOT in the table |
| Ch 3.7 - Program Buffer | `progbuf` + `postexec`, how data0..N pass values | The mechanism from my previous answer |
| Appendix A - "Debugger Implementation" | Step-by-step pseudocode for read/write GPR, CSR, memory | Shows you exactly what GDB/OpenOCD does |
| Appendix B - Example sequences | Worked JTAG bit sequences | Useful when debugging your own DTM |

Skip on first read: Sec 3.8 (System Bus Access), Sec 3.12 (Authentication), Ch 5 (Trace).

**Complementary references**

- **OpenOCD source** is the best "executable spec": `src/target/riscv/riscv-013.c` in `https://github.com/openocd-org/openocd`. Search functions like `register_read_abstract`, `register_write_abstract`, `riscv013_get_register_buf` (this last one is the vector-register progbuf sequence I described).
- **Andes-specific**: any extensions (e.g., vendor abstract commands) live in Andes' "AndeStar Privileged Spec" / "AndesCore Debug Support" PDFs - ask your Andes FAE or check the internal docs portal. The codebase has `external/andes_clarity` which may contain related headers.
- **Glossary cheat sheet** (memorize these 6 terms first):
  - **DTM** - Debug Transport Module (the JTAG TAP)
  - **DMI** - Debug Module Interface (bus between DTM and DM, here AHB)
  - **DM** - Debug Module (the actual debug logic inside the chip)
  - **Hart** - hardware thread (one CPU)
  - **Abstract Command** - DM's pre-baked API for simple reg access
  - **Program Buffer (progbuf)** - DM's "run this code on the hart" mechanism

**Practical tip**: open the spec PDF and `Vax45mpv_premium___024root.h` side-by-side. Every `dmi_*` AHB transaction in the trace will map to one of the DM registers in Ch 3.14 - that is the fastest way to make the spec "click".
